content_html:
MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 1 - Part 4 - Engaging customers and managing customer relationships
Chapter 1 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

Part 4 - Engaging customers and managing customer relationships

Learning Objective 4 – we will learn to >>>
1.4 Discuss customer relationship management and identify strategies for creating value for customers and capturing value from customers in return.

Engaging customers and managing customer relationships (1 of 8)
Customer relationship management
In its broadest sense, customer relationship management is the overall process of building and maintaining profitable customer relationships by delivering superior customer value and satisfaction.
More specifically, customer relationship management (CRM) is the process of managing detailed information about individual customers and carefully managing customer touch points in order to maximise customer loyalty.

Engaging customers and managing customer relationships (2 of 8)
Customer relationship management (cont.)
Relationship building blocks: Customer value and satisfaction
Customer-perceived value is the customer’s evaluation of the difference between all the benefits and all the costs of a market offering relative to those of competing offers.
Customer satisfaction is the extent to which a product’s perceived performance matches or exceeds a buyer’s expectations.

Engaging customers and managing customer relationships (3 of 8)
Customer relationship management (cont.)
Customer relationship levels and tools
Marketing organisations can build customer relationships at many levels, depending on the nature of the target market.
At one extreme, company with many low-margin customers may seek to develop basic relationships with them.
At the other extreme, in markets with few customers and high margins, sellers want to create full partnerships with key customers.
Q: Why might a marketing organisation choose to target low-margin customers rather than high-margin customers?

Engaging customers and managing customer relationships (4 of 8)
Customer relationship levels and tools (cont.)
Marketers can use specific marketing tools to develop stronger bonds with consumers. For example:
frequency marketing programs reward customers who buy frequently or in large amounts
club marketing programs offer members special benefits and create member communities.
Q: Do you belong to any frequency marketing programs and/or club marketing programs. Are they ‘worth it’? Why, or why not?

Engaging customers and managing customer relationships (5 of 8)
Engaging customers
Profound changes continue to occur in the ways in which companies are relating to their customers.
Yesterday’s marketing organisations focused on mass marketing to all customers at arm’s length.
Today’s companies focus is to build deeper, more direct and lasting relationships with more carefully selected customers.
Q: Can you think of some reasons for such a shift in the way companies relate to their customers?

Engaging customers and managing customer relationships (6 of 8)
Engaging customers (cont.)
Customer engagement and today’s digital and social media
Customer-engagement marketing involves fostering direct and continuous customer involvement in shaping brand conversations, brand experiences and brand community.
In customer-managed relationships, customers connect with companies and with each other to help forge their own brand experiences and, hopefully, go beyond to become brand advocates.
Q: What is a ‘brand advocate’? Give some examples. Why is brand advocacy so important to marketers?

Engaging customers and managing customer relationships (7 of 8)
Customer engagement and today’s digital and social media (cont.)
Most marketers now combine their mass-media marketing efforts with a rich mix of:
online marketing
mobile marketing
social media marketing.
Q: ‘Greater consumer empowerment means companies can no longer rely on marketing by intrusion. Instead, they must practise marketing by attraction.’ What does this mean in practice? Give some examples.

Engaging customers and managing customer relationships (8 of 8)
Consumer-generated marketing
Consumers are playing an increasing role in shaping their own brand experiences and those of other consumers through:
consumer-generated videos
blogs
websites.
Partner relationship marketing
Partner relationship management involves working closely with partners in other company departments and outside the company to jointly bring greater value to customers.

Capturing value from customers (1 of 4)
Creating customer loyalty and retention
Good customer relationship management creates customer satisfaction. In turn, satisfied customers remain loyal and talk favourably to others about the company and its products.
Customer lifetime value is the amount by which revenues from a customer over time exceed the company’s costs of attracting, selling and servicing that customer.
It is the value of the entire stream of purchases that the customer would make over a lifetime of patronage.

Capturing value from customers (2 of 4)
Growing share of customer
Share of customer is the portion of the customer’s purchasing that a company gets in its product categories.
Building customer equity
Customer equity is the total combined customer lifetime values of all of the company’s customers.

Capturing value from customers (3 of 4)
Building the right relationship with the right customers
Companies should:
manage customer equity carefully
view customers as assets that need to be managed and maximised.
Not all customers, not even all loyal customers, are good investments.
Q: Give some reasons why loyal customers are not always a ‘good investment’.

Capturing value from customers (4 of 4)
Building the right relationship with the right customers (cont.)
The company can classify customers according to their potential profitability and manage its relationships with them accordingly.
Customers can be classified into one of four relationship groups, according to their profitability and projected loyalty.
‘Strangers’ show low potential profitability and little projected loyalty.
‘Butterflies’ are potentially profitable but not loyal.
‘True friends’ are both profitable and loyal.
‘Barnacles’ are highly loyal but not very profitable.

Engaging customers and managing customer relationships
Q: Why might a marketing organisation choose to target low-margin customers rather than high-margin customers?
A: If there is a large number of low-margin customers who require only basic relationships, then the costs of maintaining those relationship would be low. Therefore they would be profitable to serve, and there would be economies of scale.
On the other hand, if high-margin customers demand in-depth relationships, they would be expensive to serve, and in addition would require customer management staff and systems which the marketer might not possess and would be expensive to acquire and maintain.

Engaging customers and managing customer relationships
Q: Do you belong to any frequency marketing programs and/or club marketing programs. Are they ‘worth it’? Why, or why not?
A: Consumers need to balance the benefits with costs – for example is there a fee to belong to the frequency program? Some consumers want to “belong” to groups and receive news and chat with other consumers. Other consumers do not want that type of “relationship” with the brand or with other consumers.

Engaging customers and managing customer relationships
Engaging customers
Yesterday’s marketing organisations focused on mass marketing to all customers at arm’s length.
Today’s companies focus is to build deeper, more direct and lasting relationships with more carefully selected customers.
Q: Can you think of some reasons for such a shift in the way companies relate to their customers?
A: Today customers have more needs and different needs than in the past, because our society is more diverse. Therefore, marketers can no longer appeal to all customers in the same way with the same mass-marketing program.

Engaging customers and managing customer relationships
Q: What is a ‘brand advocate’? Give some examples. Why is brand advocacy so important to marketers?
A: A brand advocate recommends a brand to others – friends, family, people in the same chat group, class, club and so on. Brand advocacy is important to marketers because (1) their advocacy is at no-cost to the marketer and (2) people are more likely to believe information about products from someone they know. This is especially true when the product that is being “advocated” is one that the “advocate” is seen as being an expert in, and which both people use – for example tennis products being advocated by a fellow member of the tennis club.

Engaging customers and managing customer relationships
Q: ‘Greater consumer empowerment means companies can no longer rely on marketing by intrusion. Instead, they must practise marketing by attraction.’ What does this mean in practice? Give some examples.
A: Mass marketing by means such as TV commercials and online pop-ups “intrudes” into the consumer’s life. The marketing message might not be relevant to the recipient and might annoy them. On the other hand, if the consumer is “attracted” to information about the product that they find valuable and interesting then they will find this information valuable and persuasive.

1.4 Discuss customer relationship management and identify strategies for creating value for customers and capturing value from customers in return.
